def clean_filename(filename):
    s = re.sub('[\\\\:\'\\[\\]/",<>&^$+*?;]', '_', filename)
    # deny special file names (Windows specific):
    s = re.sub('^(CON|PRN|AUX|NUL|COM\\d|LPT\\d)$', 'SPECIAL', s, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    s = s if s != '' else 'empty'
    return s

1. zdaemon starts up fine and silently and repeatedly tries to start the program in the background (this is happening on Python 3.5)
2. zdaemon fails to start up (this is happening on Python <= 3.4)
3. zdaemon hangs forever (this is what was happening on Python <= 3.5 before the workaround that this test is trying to test)

This is a matter of style that we have discussed before. If the line breaks on a function's arguments then please format it like:

```python
def __init__(
    self,
    blockchainservice,
    discovery_contract_address,
    poll_timeout=DEFAULT_POLL_TIMEOUT
):
```

Why? It's actually quite simple. First it looks symmetric, simple and organized. But for a more practical reason, when refactoring the code later and adding/removing arguments you just need to change one entry and don't have to mess with rearranging the entire arguments line.

Google style prefers the no-get style: https://google.github.io/styleguide/cppguide.html#Function_Names, but it's not required.
> Accessors and mutators (get and set functions) may be named like variables. 

Most code in Ray follows this convention, which I think it good. But there's an exception in this PR. in `Worker` class, I named the getter function of `language_` as `GetLanguage`. The reason is because if I name it `Language`, it will override the definition of `enum class Language`.
